investigation.hypothesis: The source IP 185.220.101.47 is a Tor exit node actively conducting credential brute-force attacks against SSH services. The 847 failed authentication attempts in 180 seconds targeting common system usernames (root, admin, ubuntu, oracle, postgres, git) represents a deliberate password-spray attack. The IP's reputation data shows 92 historical abuse reports with pattern consistency: brute-force SSH attacks, unauthorized connection attempts, and Plesk panel login attempts dating back to mid-April 2026.
attack_techniques: T1110.001 - Brute Force: Password Guessing (847 SSH auth failures in 180s against multiple default/common usernames)
T1110.003 - Brute Force: Password Spraying (systematic attempt against multiple accounts: root, admin, ubuntu, oracle, postgres, git)
T1021.004 - Remote Services: SSH (destination port 22, sshd service targeted)
T1078.001 - Valid Accounts: Default Accounts (targeting hardcoded system usernames)

suggested_pivots.action: Query firewall/WAF logs for other connection attempts from 185.220.101.47 to prod-web-02.internal or other internal hosts in same time window
suggested_pivots.rationale: Determine scope of attack — whether this was isolated to SSH/port 22 or part of broader network reconnaissance against multiple services/ports

suggested_pivots.action: Check auth logs on prod-web-02.internal for any SUCCESSFUL authentications from 185.220.101.47, even if rare
suggested_pivots.rationale: Confirm no credential compromise occurred despite 847 failures. Even one successful login would indicate potential lateral movement or data exfil risk

suggested_pivots.action: Correlate abuse reports mentioning 'ir-tc.com' (DirectAdmin Auto Report) and 'for-privacy.net' domain with internal security incidents or customer complaints
suggested_pivots.rationale: Recent reports mention brute-force attacks on DirectAdmin panels; if your organization or customers use DirectAdmin, this IP may have targeted them

suggested_pivots.action: Verify SSH service hardening on prod-web-02.internal: password auth disabled, key-only auth, non-standard port, fail2ban/rate-limiting active
suggested_pivots.rationale: 847 attempts in 3 minutes should have been rate-limited or auto-blocked. If it wasn't, configuration gaps may exist on this or other production hosts

suggested_pivots.action: Cross-reference Tor exit node list (Project Tor, abuse.net) to confirm 185.220.101.47 is active exit node and monitor for future attacks from this or related exit IPs
suggested_pivots.rationale: Attacker is using Tor to mask origin. Other exits from same infrastructure may conduct similar attacks; establishing attribution patterns helps with threat intelligence

investigation.escalation_draft: SECURITY INCIDENT: Confirmed brute-force attack (T1110.001, T1110.003) on prod-web-02.internal SSH service from Tor exit node 185.220.101.47 (for-privacy.net). Attack profile: 847 failed auth attempts in 180 seconds targeting default/common usernames (root, admin, ubuntu, oracle, postgres, git). Source IP has abuse score 100/100 with 92 historical reports dating to 2026-04-14, showing consistent pattern of credential attacks across multiple targets. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) Verify no successful authentications occurred on prod-web-02.internal from this IP; (2) Confirm SSH hardening (key-only auth, rate-limiting, non-standard port) is active; (3) Scan prod-web-02.internal for signs of compromise or lateral movement; (4) Check if other internal hosts were targeted in same time window; (5) Review Tor exit node blocklists and consider implementing at firewall. RISK: If password auth is enabled or rate-limiting is weak, credential compromise is possible. Escalate to Infrastructure Security and Network Operations for immediate rate-limit enforcement and threat intelligence correlation.
investigation.reasoning_transcript:
EVIDENCE GATHERING: The alert reports 847 failed SSH authentications from 185.220.101.47 to prod-web-02.internal over 180 seconds (3 minutes), targeting six common system usernames. IP reputation check reveals: (1) Abuse confidence score is 100/100 (maximum), (2) Identified as active Tor exit node (for-privacy.net), (3) 92 total abuse reports on record, (4) Recent reports span 2026-04-14 through 2026-04-22, showing sustained malicious activity, (5) Report comments consistently mention brute-force, password-spray, and unauthorized login attempts, (6) Pattern includes SSH invalid user attempts and Plesk panel login attacks.

HYPOTHESIS FORMATION: This is a credential brute-force attack. The 847 failed attempts + multiple username targets + Tor origin + maximal abuse reputation + historical pattern = strong indicators of deliberate password-spraying behavior.

MITRE MAPPING: T1110.001 (Brute Force: Password Guessing) applies directly — the alert shows systematic enumeration of authentication failures. T1110.003 (Password Spraying) is the specific variant — 847 attempts across 6 different accounts in 180 seconds fits spray pattern. T1021.004 (Remote Services: SSH) is the delivery mechanism (port 22, sshd). T1078.001 (Valid Accounts: Default Accounts) applies because the targeted usernames are system defaults (root, admin, ubuntu, oracle, postgres, git). None of these require the attacker to have succeeded; the BEHAVIOR (delivery mechanism, execution chain) maps to MITRE regardless of password outcome.

RISK ASSESSMENT: High confidence true positive. The attack behavior is unambiguous (credential spray), the source is known-malicious (Tor exit, 100/100 abuse score, 92 reports), and the target is a production web server. The critical unknown is whether the 847 failures were ALL failures or whether some subset succeeded. Escalation is warranted because: (1) Multiple MITRE techniques chain together (T1110.001 → T1110.003 → T1021.004 → potential T1078.001 compromise), (2) Target is production infrastructure, (3) SSH is a crown-jewel attack surface for lateral movement, (4) Evidence is clear but impact (successful compromise) is unconfirmed and must be ruled out immediately.

SUGGESTED PIVOTS: High-priority actions focus on confirming no successful authentication occurred and hardening the SSH service to prevent future attacks. Medium-priority actions address threat intelligence and scope validation (were other hosts attacked?). All pivots support either containment or post-incident analysis.
